perturbation.original_text: The Confidant must take all reasonable precautions to avoid unauthorised disclosure and protect against unauthorised use o f the Confidential Information , such precautions to be at least as stringent as those used by the Confidant for the protection of the Confidant ’s own confidential information and always in accordance with the Privacy Act 1988 .
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidant must take all reasonable precautions to avoid unauthorised disclosure and protect against unauthorised use o f the Confidential Information , such precautions to be significantly less stringent than those used by the Confidant for the protection of the Confidant’s own confidential information and without regard to the Privacy Act 1988.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the confidentiality standards to be 'significantly less stringent' and disregarding the Privacy Act introduces a legal contradiction because it fails to meet the minimum security standards required by Australian privacy law for handling personal information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Act 1988 - Australian Privacy Principles
perturbation.law_citation: Australian Privacy Principle 11 - Security of personal information
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11 requires organizations to take active steps to protect personal information from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. Reducing security measures below the confidant's own standards and disregarding the Privacy Act directly contradicts this obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: anagement of personal information Ensures that APP entities manage personal information in an open and transparent way. This includes having a clearly expressed and up to date APP privacy policy . APP 2 Anonymity and pseudonymity Requires APP entities to give individuals the option of not identifying themselves, or of using a pseudonym. Limited exceptions apply. APP 3 Collection of solicited personal information Outlines when an APP entity can collect personal information that is solicited. It applies higher standards to the collection of sensitive information . APP 4 Dealing with unsolicited personal information Outlines how APP entities must deal with unsolicited personal information. APP 5 Notification of the collection of personal information Outlines when and in what circumstances an APP entity that collects personal information must tell an individual about certain matters. APP 6 Use or disclosure of personal information Outlines the circumstances in which an APP entity may use or disclose personal information that it holds. APP 7 Direct marketing An organisation may only use or disclose personal information for direct marketing purposes if certain conditions are met. APP 8 Cross-border disclosure of personal information Outlines the steps an APP entity must take to protect personal information before it is disclosed overseas. APP 9 Adoption, use or disclosure of government related identifiers Outlines the limited circumstances when an organisation may adopt a government related identifier of an individual as its own identifier, or use or disclose a government related identifier of an individual. APP 10 Quality of personal information An APP entity must take reasonable steps to ensure the personal information it collects is accurate, up to date and complete. An entity must also take reasonable steps to ensure the personal information it uses or discloses is accurate, up to date, complete and relevant, having regard to the purpose of the use or disclosure. APP 11 Security of personal information An APP entity must take reasonable steps to protect personal information it holds from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. An entity has obligations to destroy or de-identify personal information in certain circumstances. APP 12 Access to personal information Outlines an APP entity’s obligations when an individual requests to be given access to personal information held about them by the entity. This includes a requirement to provide access unless a specific exception applies. APP 13 Correction of personal information Outlines an APP entity’s obligations in relation to correcting the personal information it holds about individuals. perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly states that precautions will be 'significantly less stringent' than the confidant's own confidential information practices and 'without regard to the Privacy Act 1988.' This directly contradicts APP 11, which requires entities to 'take reasonable steps to protect personal information it holds from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure.' By intentionally reducing security measures and disregarding the Privacy Act, the changed text creates a clear contradiction by failing to meet the required standards for protecting personal information as outlined in APP 11.

perturbation.original_text: Following any request by NostraData , the Confidant must immediately provide to NostraData all Confidential Information and certify that no Confidential Information is retained by the Confidant .
perturbation.changed_text: Following any request by NostraData, the Confidant must provide to NostraData all Confidential Information within 90 days and is not required to certify whether any Confidential Information is retained.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the deadline for returning confidential information to 'within 90 days' and removing the certification requirement contradicts the principle of immediate return of data upon request, potentially violating data protection obligations under the Privacy Act by delaying access and control over personal data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Act 1988 - Australian Privacy Principles
perturbation.law_citation: Australian Privacy Principle 12 - Access to personal information
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 12 mandates that organizations give individuals access to their personal information within a reasonable timeframe. While this clause addresses an organisation's obligations to the data subject, the contract modification directly impacts an organisation's ability to meet those obligations as it delays the return of information when the provider requests it.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a contradiction because it changes the requirement for immediate return of confidential information to 'within 90 days' and removes the certification requirement. The original contract ensures immediate compliance with potential data protection obligations. APP 12, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, outlines an entity’s obligations when an individual requests to be given access to personal information held about them by the entity. The law explanation states that the contract modification directly impacts an organisation's ability to meet those obligations as it delays the return of information when the provider requests it. This delay and the removal of the certification, weaken the data protection, making the changed text non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: At the conclusion of the Datathon the Confi dant must permanently delete all data provided by NostraData . The data may be retained if NostraData makes specific requests for further demonstrations.
perturbation.changed_text: At the conclusion of the Datathon the Confidant may retain all data provided by NostraData indefinitely, even without specific requests from NostraData.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing indefinite retention of data without consent contradicts the data minimisation and storage limitation principles under the Privacy Act, which requires data to be held only as long as necessary for its intended purpose.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Act 1988 - Australian Privacy Principles
perturbation.law_citation: Australian Privacy Principle 11 - Security of personal information
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11 obligates organizations to protect personal information and to destroy or de-identify personal information when it is no longer needed for any purpose for which the information may be used or disclosed under the APPs. Indefinite retention directly violates this principle.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the Confidant to retain data indefinitely without NostraData's consent, which directly contradicts APP 11's requirement to destroy or de-identify personal information when it's no longer needed. The law snippet states that an entity has obligations to 'destroy or de-identify personal information in certain circumstances', making indefinite retention a clear violation.

perturbation.original_text: By making a submi ssion at the conclusion of the D atathon , consisting of a deck of slides presenting the Confidant's insights extracted from the Confidential Information, the Confidant grants the Datathon organisers and NostraData an irrevocable, non -exclusive, royalty -free and perpetual right to use, modify, di stribute to the public, and publicly display the content of the submission
perturbation.changed_text: By making a submission at the conclusion of the Datathon, consisting of a deck of slides presenting the Confidant's insights extracted from the Confidential Information, the Confidant grants the Datathon organisers and NostraData an irrevocable, exclusive, royalty-free and perpetual right to use, modify, distribute to the public, and publicly display the content of the submission, and further grants NostraData the right to claim intellectual property rights over the submission.
perturbation.explanation: Granting NostraData an 'exclusive' right and allowing them to claim intellectual property over the Confidant's submission contradicts the implicit understanding that the Confidant retains ownership of their intellectual property. This may also violate fair dealing provisions and principles relating to intellectual property assignment.
perturbation.law_citation: Copyright Act 1968 - Section 196: Assignment of copyright
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 196 of the Copyright Act 1968 dictates the requirements for copyright assignment. The modified text suggests an assignment of copyright without proper consideration or explicit written agreement, as well as creating the ability for NostraData to take IP rights over submissions.

perturbation.justification: The changed text grants NostraData the exclusive right to use, modify, distribute, and publicly display the content of the submission, and further grants NostraData the right to claim intellectual property rights over the submission. The Law Explanation says this contradicts Section 196 of the Copyright Act 1968, because it dictates the requirements for copyright assignment, and suggests the modified text creates the ability for NostraData to take IP rights over submissions, without proper consideration or explicit written agreement. The Perturbation explanation also says this violates fair dealing provisions and principles relating to intellectual property assignment. Therefore, a contradiction exists because the changed text allows NostraData to claim intellectual property rights that should remain with the Confidant, potentially violating copyright law.

perturbation.original_text: This deed is governed by the laws of the State of Victoria , Australia .
perturbation.changed_text: This deed is governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, USA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, USA, for a contract explicitly related to an Australian Datathon and involving Australian parties creates a contradiction. The contract's subject matter and parties have a closer connection to Victoria, Australia, making the choice of Delaware law potentially unenforceable under conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.law_citation: Choice of Law Rules in Contract (Australia)
perturbation.law_explanation: Australian conflict of law principles dictate that the law governing a contract should be the law of the jurisdiction with which the contract has the closest and most real connection. A contract between Australian parties concerning an event in Australia is unlikely to be governed by Delaware law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law from Victoria, Australia, to Delaware, USA. The Law Explanation states that Australian conflict of law principles dictate that the law governing a contract should be the law of the jurisdiction with which the contract has the closest and most real connection. The Perturbation Explanation also suggests that the contract's subject matter and parties have a closer connection to Victoria, Australia. The 'conflict of laws' snippet indicates that a court determines what law to apply in accordance with the law of the forum. Changing the governing law to Delaware contradicts the principle that the contract should be governed by the jurisdiction with the closest connection, creating a conflict of law issue and making the contract potentially unenforceable, thus a legal contradiction exists.
